name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Parece que esta funcionando bien.

sideB.text: It seems to be working fine.
It looks like it's working well.
It appears to be working correctly.
It seems it's working fine.

cards.sideA: Parece que estuviera funcionando mal.

sideB.text: It seems to be malfunctioning.
It looks like it's working incorrectly.
It appears to be acting up.
It seems to be working badly.
It seems to be working incorrectly.

cards.note:
**[208]**

~It seems to be working wrong.~ and ~It seems it's working wrong.~ are unnatural. For devices, systems, or code, use [malfunctioning], [working incorrectly], or [acting up].

~It looks like to be working incorrectly.~ is grammatically invalid. The phrase 'looks like' must be followed by a full clause (e.g., 'looks like it is working...'), not an infinitive.

While ~working wrong~ might be heard in highly informal or non-native speech, 'wrong' is an adjective, not an adverb. The correct adverbial form is [incorrectly]. In professional and standard American English, mechanical or software failures are described using [malfunctioning] or [acting up]. Using 'working wrong' sounds unprofessional and is grammatically flawed.

~It seems to be working bad.~ is grammatically invalid because [bad] is an adjective and cannot modify a verb. You must use an adverb like [badly] or [poorly], though [malfunctioning] is the standard term in technical contexts.

~It seems to be badly.~ is grammatically invalid. The verb 'to be' is a linking verb and must be followed by an adjective (like [bad]), not an adverb. If you want to use the adverb [badly], it must modify an action verb, as in [working badly].

~It seems to be working wrong.~ is addressed above. 'Wrong' is an adjective, so you must use the adverb [incorrectly] or [badly] to modify the verb [working].

cards.sideA: Un segundo tiene 1.000 milliseconds o ms.

sideB.text: One second has 1,000 milliseconds or ms.
There are 1,000 milliseconds, or ms, in a second.
A single second contains 1,000 milliseconds.

cards.sideA: 1500 ms es 1500 milliseconds, eso equivale a 1.5 segundos.

sideB.text: 1,500 ms is 1,500 milliseconds, which is equivalent to 1.5 seconds.
1500 ms means 1500 milliseconds, equaling 1.5 seconds.
1500 milliseconds, or ms, equals 1.5 seconds.

cards.note:
**[34]**



~..., that is equivalent to 1.5 seconds.~ uses incorrect relative pronoun grammar. A non-restrictive relative clause following a comma must use [which], never 'that'.

cards.sideA: IBE es el codigo de Oracle iStore.

sideB.text: IBE is the Oracle iStore code.
IBE stands for the Oracle iStore code.
The code for Oracle iStore is IBE.

cards.sideA: No esta devolviendo los datos.

sideB.text: It's not returning the data.
It isn't sending back the data.
The data is not being returned.

cards.note: ~It's not retrieving the data.~ changes the meaning. The verb [retrieve] means to fetch or get data (recuperar/obtener), whereas the original sentence specifies that the system is not giving the data back (devolver). Use [returning] or [sending back].

cards.sideA: Sorry, soy un poco lento haciendo esto manualmente.

sideB.text: Sorry, I'm a bit slow doing this manually.
I apologize, I'm a little slow doing this by hand.
Sorry, doing this manually takes me a bit longer.
Sorry, I'm a bit slow doing it this way.

cards.sideA: Soy un poco lento haciendolo de esta forma.

sideB.text: I'm a bit slow doing it this way.
I'm a little slow doing it like this.
Doing it this way makes me a bit slow.
I'm a bit slow doing this this way.

cards.sideA: En el camino suelen ir apareciendo errores.

sideB.text: Errors usually pop up along the way.
Bugs tend to appear along the way.
Mistakes usually show up as we go.
Errors usually appear along the way.
Errors normally appear along the way.

cards.note:
**[207]**

~Bugs tend to appear on the way.~ is physically directional. For processes or projects, [along the way] is the natural collocation.

~Bugs tend to appear in the way.~ means they are acting as an obstacle blocking your path, not that they happen during the process.

~Errors usually appears along the way.~ is grammatically incorrect. 'Errors' is plural, so the verb must be [appear].

~Errors appears usually along the way.~ has incorrect subject-verb agreement and incorrect adverb placement. Adverbs of frequency like [usually] go before the main verb.

~Errors appear normally along the way.~ is awkward. Placing 'normally' after the verb makes it sound like the *manner* of appearance is normal, rather than the frequency. Use adverbs of frequency like [usually] or [normally] before the main verb: [Errors normally appear].

cards.sideA: En el camino lo aprenderemos.

sideB.text: We'll learn it along the way.
We will learn it as we go.
We'll pick it up along the way.

cards.note:
**[207]**

~We'll learn it on the way.~ implies learning during a physical trip. For acquiring skills during a project or process, use [along the way].

~We'll learn it in the way.~ is incorrect because 'in the way' means blocking a physical path or obstructing progress.

cards.sideA: En el camino te lo hare saber.

sideB.text: I'll let you know along the way.
I will tell you as we go.
I'll inform you along the way.

cards.note:
**[207]**

~I'll let you know on the way.~ is only valid if you are physically traveling to a destination. For a project or process, use [along the way].

~I'll let you know in the way.~ is incorrect because 'in the way' means obstructing or blocking a path.

cards.sideA: Por favor, infórmanos una vez que esté arriba.

sideB.text: Please let us know once it is up.
Please inform us once it's up.
Please notify us when it is up.

cards.sideA: Por favor, avísanos cuando esté en funcionamiento.

sideB.text: Please notify us once it is operational.
Please let us know once it is running.
Please inform us when it's up and running.
Please notify us once it is working.

cards.sideA: Hoy dia envie el .war por correo a Dimitri.

sideB.text: I sent the .war file to Dimitri by email today.
Today I emailed the .war file to Dimitri.
I emailed Dimitri the .war file today.

cards.sideA: Envie el .war por correo a Dimitri para ser deployado.

sideB.text: I sent the .war file to Dimitri by email to be deployed.
I emailed the .war file to Dimitri for deployment.
I sent the .war file via email to Dimitri so it can be deployed.

cards.sideA: Una vez podamos testearlo en blue, podemos crear el nuevo release.

sideB.text: Once we can test it in blue, we can create the new release.
As soon as we test it in the blue environment, we can create the new release.
After testing it in blue, we can generate the new release.

cards.sideA: El debe 1356 dolares.

sideB.text: He owes 1356 dollars.
He owes one thousand three hundred and fifty-six dollars.
He has a debt of 1356 dollars.

cards.sideA: Parece que ya está funcionando.

sideB.text: It seems to be working now.
It looks like it's working already.
It appears to be functioning now.
It seems it's working now.

cards.sideA: Parecen tener el mismo issue.

sideB.text: They seem to have the same issue.
It looks like they have the same issue.
They appear to be experiencing the same issue.

cards.sideA: Parece que hay un delay.

sideB.text: There seems to be a delay.
It looks like there's a delay.
It appears there is a delay.
It seems there's a delay.

cards.sideA: Todo parece estar en orden.

sideB.text: Everything seems to be in order.
It looks like everything is in order.
Everything appears to be fine.
All seems to be in order.

cards.sideA: Nuestros ingresos mensuales están aumentando.

sideB.text: Our monthly revenue is increasing.
Our monthly income is going up.
Our revenue per month is growing.

cards.sideA: La empresa reportó ingresos récord este año.

sideB.text: The company reported record revenue this year.
The business announced record revenue this year.
The company posted record earnings this year.

cards.note:
**[206]**

The word [record] has multiple meanings based on its function. As a noun, it means a documented history or file (un registro). As an adjective (as used in this context), it means the best or highest ever achieved (cifra récord). As a verb, it means to capture audio, video, or data (grabar/registrar).

cards.sideA: Necesitamos maximizar nuestra utilidad neta.

sideB.text: We need to maximize our net profit.
We must maximize our net income.
We have to maximize our bottom line.
We need to maximize our net income.

cards.note:
**[206]**

[Revenue] is the total amount of money brought in by a company's operations (ventas/ingresos brutos). [Profit] is the financial gain after all operating expenses, taxes, and costs have been deducted from the revenue (utilidad/ganancia neta).

~We need to maximize our net utility.~ is a false friend error. In English finance, [utility] refers to usefulness or public services (water, electricity). The Spanish 'utilidad' translates to [profit] or [income].

cards.sideA: Los altos costos redujeron nuestra ganancia total.

sideB.text: The high costs reduced our total profit.
High costs cut into our total profit.
The total profit was reduced by the high costs.

cards.sideA: Los ingresos es lo que recaudas, la ganancia es lo que conservas.

sideB.text: Revenue is what you collect, profit is what you keep.
Revenue is the money you bring in, profit is what stays.
Income is what you collect, whereas profit is what you keep.
Revenue is what you get, profit is what you keep.
Revenue is what you receive, profit is what you keep.

cards.sideA: Por favor, verifique la dirección de facturación en esta factura.

sideB.text: Please verify the BILL TO address on this invoice.
Kindly check the billing address on this invoice.
Please double-check the billing address on this invoice.

cards.note:
**[205]**

~Please verify the invoice address on this invoice.~ is unnatural due to the repetition of 'invoice'. Use 'billing address' or 'BILL TO address' instead.

~Please verify that the BILL TO address on this invoice.~ is missing a verb to complete the clause (e.g., 'is correct').

~Please verify the BILL TO address is on this invoice.~ is grammatically correct but changes the meaning to verifying the *presence* of the address rather than the address itself.

~Please verify the BILL TO address in this invoice.~ uses the wrong preposition. Information is printed or written [on] a document, page, or surface, not 'in' it.

cards.sideA: Los datos del solicitante y de facturación son los mismos.

sideB.text: The SOLD TO and BILL TO information is the same.
The ordering and billing details are identical.
The buyer and billing details are one and the same.
The SOLD TO and BILL TO are the same information.

cards.note:
**[205]**



~The SOLD TO and BILL TO is the same information.~ has a subject-verb agreement error. Since you are referring to two distinct items, the verb must be plural: [are the same information]. However, stating [information is the same] is much more natural.

cards.sideA: Cambie el campo de solicitante por nuestra sucursal regional.

sideB.text: Change the SOLD TO field to our regional branch.
Update the ordering party to our regional office.
Please switch the "Sold To" party to our regional branch.

cards.note:
**[205]**

n~Change the SOLD TO field for our regional branch.~ uses the wrong preposition. When updating the value of a field or variable, use [change to]. 'Change for' implies a physical swap or exchange.

cards.sideA: Oficina corporativa para facturación y bodega para despacho.

sideB.text: Corporate for BILL TO and warehouse for SHIP TO.
Bill to corporate; ship to warehouse.
Invoice the corporate office and deliver to the warehouse.
Corporate for BILL TO and warehouse for shipping.

cards.note:
**[205]**

~Corporate for invoice and warehouse for SHIP TO.~ breaks the standard terminology pairing. In logistics and accounting, [BILL TO] is strictly paired with [SHIP TO].

~Corporative for BILL TO and warehouse for SHIP TO.~ is invalid because [corporative] is a rare adjective, not a noun. Use [corporate] to refer to the corporate office.

cards.sideA: Necesito una guía rápida para la nueva sintaxis.

sideB.text: I need a cheatsheet for the new syntax.
I require a quick reference guide for the new syntax.
I need a quick guide for the new syntax.

cards.sideA: Tengo una guía rápida para las consultas de la BD.

sideB.text: I have a DB query cheatsheet.
I have a quick guide for the database queries.
I have a quick reference guide for DB queries.
I have a cheatsheet for the db queries.

cards.sideA: Todavía le debo mucho dinero al banco.

sideB.text: I still owe a lot of money to the bank.
I still owe the bank a lot of money.
I still owe a large amount to the bank.

cards.sideA: Adquiriremos los nuevos servidores el próximo mes.

sideB.text: We will purchase the new servers next month.
We are going to purchase the new servers next month.
We plan to purchase the new servers next month.
We will buy the new servers next month.
We will acquire the new servers next month.

cards.note:
**[203]**



~We will adquiere the new servers next month.~ contains a spelling error. The correct English spelling is [acquire].

~We will acquire the new servers for next month.~ implies the servers are intended to be used next month, not that the purchase itself will happen then.

~We will acquire the new servers in next month.~ is grammatically incorrect. Do not use prepositions like [in] or [on] before [next] or [last] when indicating time.

~We will purchase the new servers for next month.~ is incorrect because the preposition [for] indicates purpose or a deadline rather than the specific time an action occurs. To state when the purchase happens, use [next month] directly without a preposition.

cards.sideA: La actualización del software causó un retroceso funcional.

sideB.text: The software update caused a functional regression.
A functional regression was caused by the software update.
The update to the software resulted in a functional regression.

cards.note:
**[204]**



~The software updating caused a functional regression.~ is unnatural. The standard noun form for a new release of software is [update], not the gerund 'updating'.

cards.sideA: El recorte de presupuesto fue un gran contratiempo para el equipo.

sideB.text: A budget cut was a major setback for the team.
The budget cut acted as a huge setback for the team.
The team experienced a major setback due to the budget cut.
A budget cut was a big setback for the team.
A budget cut was a huge setback for the team.

cards.note:
**[204]**



~A cut of the budget was a major setback for the team.~ is unnatural. Use the noun adjunct [budget cut] for efficiency.

~A budget cut was a major step back for the team.~ changes the meaning. A [step back] is a deliberate pause or retreat to gain perspective. A [setback] is an involuntary obstacle or delay.

~A budget cut was a major back step for the team.~ is not a standard English expression. Use [setback] for an involuntary obstacle or [step back] for a deliberate retreat.

cards.sideA: El presupuesto no ha sido aprobado aun.

sideB.text: The budget hasn't been approved yet.
The budget has not yet been approved.
The budget isn't approved yet.

cards.sideA: El presupuesto ya ha sido rechazado.

sideB.text: The budget has already been rejected.
The budget was already rejected.
The budget is already rejected.
The budget has been rejected already.

cards.note:
**[206]**



~The budget has been already rejected.~ is unnatural. The adverb 'already' should be placed after the first auxiliary verb ([has already been]) or at the very end of the sentence ([has been rejected already]).

cards.sideA: Nop, tampoco funciona para mí.

sideB.text: Nope, doesn't work for me either.
No, it doesn't work for me either.
Nope, it is not working for me either.

cards.sideA: Nop, tampoco lo sabía.

sideB.text: Nope, I didn't know that either.
No, I didn't know that either.
Nope, I wasn't aware of that either.

cards.sideA: Similar a como funciona ahora.

sideB.text: Similar to how it works now.
Much like how it works right now.
Close to the way it currently works.

cards.sideA: Yo supuse que no debía enviarlo.

sideB.text: I assumed I shouldn't send it.
I assumed I wasn't supposed to send it.
I figured I shouldn't send it.
I thought I shouldn't send it.
I guessed I shouldn't send it.

cards.note:
~I supposed I shouldn't send it.~ is unnatural. In English, the verb 'suppose' is rarely used as a simple past action to mean 'creer' or 'asumir'. Instead, 'suppose' is typically used for obligations (e.g., 'I was supposed to...') or hypotheticals. For the Spanish 'Yo supuse' (meaning 'I assumed' or 'I figured'), you must use [assumed], [figured], or [thought].

~I assumed I didn't have to send it.~ changes the meaning. [Didn't have to] means there was no obligation ('no tenía que'), whereas [shouldn't] means it was a bad idea or prohibited ('no debía').

cards.sideA: Yo supuse mal los requerimientos.

sideB.text: I incorrectly assumed the requirements.
I made a wrong assumption about the requirements.
I misunderstood the requirements.
I wrongly assumed the requirements.

cards.note:
~I assumed wrong the requirements.~ and ~I assumed bad the requirements.~ are grammatically invalid. An adverb (like incorrectly or wrongly) cannot be placed between the verb (assumed) and its direct object (the requirements). You must place the adverb before the verb, as in [incorrectly assumed]. Furthermore, 'bad' is an adjective; the adverb is [badly].

~I supposed bad the requirements.~ is incorrect for the same reasons, plus the verb 'suppose' is not used this way to mean 'asumir'. Use [assumed].

~I badly assumed the requirements.~ is unnatural because [badly] typically describes the quality of a performance or a severe need. To describe a factual error, use [wrongly] or [incorrectly].

cards.sideA: Mi error, mi suposición no fue correcta.

sideB.text: My bad, my assumption was incorrect.
My mistake, my assumption was wrong.
My fault, my assumption wasn't correct.
My bad, my assumption wasn't correct.
My bad, my guess wasn't correct.

cards.sideA: Como en los requerimientos no aparecía eso, no lo agregué.

sideB.text: Since that wasn't in the requirements, I didn't add it.
As it wasn't in the requirements, I didn't include it.
Because that didn't appear in the requirements, I didn't add it.

cards.note:
**[82]**



~Since in the requirements that didn't appear~ and ~Since in the requirements didn't appear that~ are grammatically invalid. In English, you must follow the strict Subject + Verb + Location/Context order. The correct structure is [Since that didn't appear in the requirements].

cards.sideA: Nuestros ingresos mensuales están disminuyendo.

sideB.text: Our monthly revenue is decreasing.
Our monthly income is going down.
Our monthly revenue is declining.

cards.sideA: Conocimos a mucha gente interesante en el camino.

sideB.text: We met a lot of interesting people along the way.
We encountered many interesting people along the way.
We got to know a lot of interesting people along the way.

cards.sideA: Recogimos algunos suministros en el camino.

sideB.text: We picked up some supplies along the way.
We grabbed some supplies along the way.
We collected some supplies along the way.

cards.note:
**[207]**



~We picked up some supplies in the way.~ is incorrect because [in the way] means something is acting as an obstacle or blocking your path (estorbando). When referring to things that happen or are collected during a journey from point A to point B, the correct phrase is [along the way]. If you are currently traveling and heading to a destination, you say you are [on the way].

cards.sideA: Él parece estar teniendo problemas con la API.

sideB.text: He seems to be struggling with the API.
He appears to be having issues with the API.
He seems to be having trouble with the API.
He seems to be having problems with the API.

cards.sideA: La base de datos parece estar respondiendo lentamente.

sideB.text: The database seems to be responding slowly.
The database appears to be answering slowly.
The DB seems to be replying slowly.

cards.sideA: Ellos afirman estar ganando dinero.

sideB.text: They claim to be making money.
They claim to be earning money.
They profess to be making money.
They say they are making money.
They say that they are making money.

cards.note:
**[208]**



~They say to be making money.~ is grammatically invalid. The verb 'say' cannot be followed by an infinitive; it must be followed by a clause, as in [They say they are making money].

~They assure to be making money.~ and ~They ensure to be making money.~ are also invalid. The verb 'assure' requires a person as an object (e.g., [They assure us that...]), and 'ensure' means to guarantee an outcome, not to claim a fact.

~They say to be making money.~ is invalid because verbs of communication like [say] require a full clause to report speech. Only specific catenative verbs, such as verbs of perception or mental state (like [seem], [appear], [claim]), have the special property of linking directly to an infinitive like [to be making].
